# Transcrição: 03-Calibration Testing

- **Fonte:** 03-Calibration Testing
- **Duração:** 16:53
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:06]

So now I want to explain to you what account calibration is and why we do it. So with Facebook, when you are testing, so let's say you're an e-commerce, generally you want to test against the pixel that has the most value for you. So if you're an e-com, you'd be testing against the purchase pixel using website conversion. If you're doing lead gem, then you're optimized against registration complete, for example, or if you're doing mobile app install, you might optimize for app installs, app registration. So generally when you're testing, you should always be testing against the pixel that has the biggest meaning to you and gives you best indication of performance. Now the problem is, and this is kind of where I came into Facebook many, many years ago, when I first was testing with Facebook, I didn't have big budgets. I was playing with $50 a day budgets, which were there for testing and scaling and everything else going up to 100, 200, etc. So when I was starting off, I didn't really have much budget to invest on ads. So I wanted to find a way, especially working with many kind of early stage startups and new accounts, of finding a balance between doing good quality testing, but also preserving my budget. So in any natural scenario, if I'm doing ecom testing, I want to test against the purchase pixel. Herein lies the problem. Often the CPM, the cost per thousand impressions is naturally more expensive for the purchase pixel than it is for something like add to car or view content or even impressions or link clicks. And it's done because the cost to reach that audience is more expensive. More people are bidding on people that are in buying mode, for example, so people that are tagged as being in that kind of purchase pixel than people that are in the add to cart or initiate checkout. So what I did about two, three years ago is come up with a formula of looking at CPMs and looking at the value of the different tests that I was running, not based on how much I'm spending, but based on impressions. If you've been through the previous videos and kind of understood about discount bidding, one thing that Facebook does is within the first 500 impressions, Facebook learns a lot about your ad. The reason is what Facebook can get from that 500 impressions and all the data points they've got, 500 is for them a significant number. So then I start thinking, right, so if I tested my ads and got 500 impressions, what could I learn from that? So as I went through the testing process, I tried 500,000, 2000, 5000 impressions. And what I actually found was the further away the pixel was from the click, the more impressions you needed to create, what I would say, statistical relevance, not quite scientific

## [03:06 - 06:06]

statistical, but enough for me to have confidence in the numbers. So here's an example. When I'm running typical lead gen, so click on ad, land on a landing page that has a registration form and then you hit the registration complete pixel. What I found is because the path from click to my main event was only like one or two clicks, so a couple of clicks, I found that 1000 impressions was enough for me to gauge whether an ad was going to be profitable or not. So if you look at this and say, right, for 1000 impressions, what is my CPM, my cost per 1000? So in some cases, my CPM was $5. In some cases, it was $50, especially if you if you play in some like make money online niches, $50 CPMs can be quite common in e-com. It can be a lot less. But what I found was if I'm testing based on impressions, my cost is therefore variable. So if I'm in a niche where my CPMs are $10, for example, that means for $10, I can reach a thousand impressions. And if I'm optimizing for registration complete and it's one click away, all I need to do is a thousand impressions to know if an ad works. So if you then look at that across your test, test planning, then I only need to spend $10 to test one ad. I need to spend $100 to test 10 ads, for example. But the way that I've seen many people look at it, this is you should always spend, you know, $50 per ad. And if it works great, if it doesn't then bin it. The problem is you probably over testing. So what I'm trying to do is find the sweet spot between spending enough to get good data on the ad, but not wasting money. Now you might look at this, and there's two sides, you might look at this and say, this is great, I have really limited testing budget. This is going to work great for me. You might also come from the other side, which is I'm spending tens of thousands of dollars a day. I don't need to save money. But what I like to do is even in that scenario is to test more rather than test with higher budgets to get a balance between tests lasting at least one or two days. Because the other part of it is, even if you've got a super high test budget, you know, you've got thousands that you can sink into testing a day. I wouldn't want to over spend on an ad because if I, if I, if I, let's say, you know, I spent $50, $100 on an ad and, you know, within half a day I got my data. The problem is I want at least one or two 24 hour cycles to see how that ad actually performs. So I'm balancing my budget first based on getting the best value for my spend, but secondly, from making sure that I get good quality data. So testing isn't always about velocity.

## [06:06 - 09:09]

So how fast you can get results. It's also about the quality of the data you're getting back. So coming back to account collaboration. This is something I do. First of all, on a brand new ad account. So this one, this ad account here, it was launched, I think a couple of months ago, and we did this calibration test at the time. I also do it as a check back when I want to look at what the CPMs are for my different events, for example, so view content, add to cart, etc. Every six months, I might just check in and just see if the CPM prices have changed, maybe by audience, by pixel event, etc. So specifically, when I'm creating an account calibration test, what I'm doing is to find the pixel event that I want to use for my graduation testing. So let me show you how we go about setting that up. So this is a CBO campaign. So there's nothing special here. It's just standard settings for the CBO. Then at the ad set level, what we do is pick one audience. If it's a brand new account, then it's based on the 5W avatar and 4Funnel to identify one of our best audiences. You can do this for multiple. If you're doing this for multiple audiences, then you'd have a separate campaign for each of your audiences. So you'll have account calibration one, two, three, for example. And then within that, what you do is you create one ad set based on that audience of targeting. So for us, we've chosen an interest here, home repair, and then you duplicate that for as many pixel events as you've got. So here, if I just expand that out, you can see that the audience is exactly the same. The only difference is the pixel event. So if you view content, ad to cart, initiate checkout, ad payment info and purchase. So this is a typical E-com setup for calibration testing. This is a Shopify store. So it's got all the standard pixels that you'd expect already set up. Now for this ad account, we wanted to test to see which pixel we should be running through grad testing. And generally when I do this, I'm actually testing up to 5,000 impressions. So I'm going way over and beyond what I would naturally get, kind of need for understanding and interpreting the data. The reason is this, what I found is for the purchase event, you need about 5,000 impressions to give you enough data to see if that event is working for you. For ad payment info, then I would probably drop that to maybe 4,000, initiate checkout, 3,000, ad to cart to view content one. These are just rough numbers based on my experience of kind of how much data you need. And it's also based on how many conversion events are happening. So for this spend here, the spends are variable because again, like I said, it's based on

## [09:09 - 12:12]

CPM. So for me to reach the impressions on ad to cart, it was more expensive than view content. For some reason, purchase was actually cheaper in terms of CPM than ad to cart, which is quite promising. There's many reasons behind that. But the main thing here is if my purchase CPMs are at a kind of good level, and actually the CPMs were similar for purchase and payment info, for this ad account, I would choose to use the purchase event. Now when I look at the data, I look across and I look at the pixel event I'm actually optimizing for, and I'm looking at what kind of costs I'm seeing. So here, and this is another thing that you should understand about the pixel and how Facebook operates. Here at the top, I've optimized for the view content event. So that set up on my product page. And Facebook is literally getting me people that will only get to that stage. Like here's the crazy thing. Facebook have literally given me window shoppers, people that will click and add, go to the product page and just not go anywhere else. I've driven a good number of clicks to this page, but they've gone nowhere else. This is natural behavior. You know what Facebook, what you're telling Facebook is, this is the important event for me. I don't really care about sales or romance. I just want people to view my product page. They may or may not be buyers. Here's the thing. If you are clicking and add and looking at sites, you're probably interested. You might be a potential customer in that niche, but you're not going to buy. So it could be that you're not in market right now, or you are just generally a window shopper. But for some reason you are clicking the ad. Then I look at the ad to cart and Facebook's giving me really good cost-proud to cart rates. Actually, the people that have added to cart have actually gone through and started the checkout process as well. So that's really promising. So not only are Facebook giving me super low ad to cart rates, they're also getting people into the checkout. Then I look at the initiate checkout pixel event. They're giving me good ad to cart rates and also great checkout initiated events as well. Now you could look at this and say, well, surely based on what I've said about view content, that's exactly what's happening here. That is that people hitting this part of the audience are the people that are more likely to initiate the checkout, but then go no further. Now there's a few things to think about with this. Number one is why would someone go to the effort of clicking an ad, finding a product and adding that to the cart, and also go to the checkout and not buy? Chances are these are people that are not in market. So it doesn't mean that they're bad users or bad clicks. It's quite possibly that they're not the people that we're buying right now. And why that's important is when you're playing with higher average order values, sometimes you do need to optimize for other pixel events for people that are out of market or are not

## [12:12 - 15:13]

naturally just going to buy impulsively or within the next 30 days. 30 days is something that Facebook looks at in terms of purchase intent. I'll give you an example. I was running ads for an e-com store. Average order value, I think was $400 or $500. We were optimizing for these kind of pixel events because we could funnel people into our retargeting audience. And then we knew that because it was a high ticket value, we really needed to nurture them. So just like when you capture a lead off Facebook and push them through your email campaigns, we're doing that through Facebook. So we were finding people that had a keen interest in the product that were actually adding to the cart and potentially starting the checkout process, but they weren't yet convinced. And we used our website retargeting audiences to convert them. Our top of funnel didn't really have much sales, but our profitability was all in our retargeting. So it's not always the case that purchase is always the best event as well. And this is part of understanding how Facebook works. So although this training is about how to set up calibration testing, I'm giving you some insight into the pixel here and how that works as well. So this one is optimized for payment info. And then I go down to purchase. Now one thing to recognize is although for purchase, my add to cart rate is $45. Check out is $45 and I've spent $45. That means I've had one person add to cart and one person, a same person, initiate checkout. My cost per product view is $1. And one thing actually we found from this is the two sides actually, the first thing is if you have one person hitting the initiate checkout stage that is part of the purchase audience, the quality of that is far greater than these because these are in market people and actually I'd say add payment info are close to being in market as well. So these audiences and these values are more valuable to me than these. So even though it's cheaper, I'm looking at this and saying, right. And this is an FYI for you, the average order value for this store that we're getting right now is $100. So if I can get people at $45 and even converting it $50, that's still profitable for us on this store. So the purchase event for testing is viable. Now what we decided to do based on the results was actually to optimize for initiate checkout. Now the reason is when it comes to graduation testing, we are not testing for people to buy the product. We are looking for people that are showing an interest in the ad. And it's really important to understand that there's a different differentiation between when we're doing our kind of standard ad testing to find people interested in ads is very different to doing product testing where we want to know if people will buy the product. This product, for example, we know people buy it. We've done research on a separate store and we brought it to this store.

## [15:13 - 16:53]

So we know the product sells like this is a different test. This is not product that does the product sell test. This is does the ad resonate with the audience. And in this case, we use initiate checkout so that in our testing, we could focus on cheaper testing because we had limited budget for this particular campaign. And then we can maximize our results. Once we'd actually generated profitability and we had more money to invest in our testing, we were actually then switching our testing to the purchase event, spending more money and getting even better validated data back as well. So that's just kind of how we set up our testing. And also as an FYI, one thing I didn't cover is, you know, one of the questions you may have is, well, what ad do you run? For this, we picked our best ad based on our 5W avatar research. So the particular angle for this product was around the benefits. And we tried to kind of push the benefits in terms of the how it would change someone's process around kind of home repair and make it easier for them, remove the stress and things like that. So this ad was copied within each of these ad sets. So it's literally this exactly the same ad. It was shared via post ID. So we've eliminated the ad as a variable. So it's the same ad. It's the same audience. The only variable here is the optimized pixel event. So that allows us to test and actually see what kind of costs are coming back, what kind of quality of data and then make a decision on which pixel to optimize for.
